---
title: LLMs
description: Making your docs LLM-friendly with auto-generated llms.txt and per-page Markdown sources.
---

`inspect-docs` makes your documentation LLM-friendly out of the box. Every render produces an [`llms.txt`](https://llmstxt.org) index at the root of your site and a plain-Markdown source for every rendered page, giving language models a structured entry point and clean raw content to work with.

## llms.txt

An [`llms.txt`](https://llmstxt.org) file is automatically generated at the root of your rendered site, following the [llmstxt.org](https://llmstxt.org) spec. It's built from your site's `title`, `description`, `navigation`, and reference pages — no configuration required.

The generated file looks roughly like this:

```markdown
# My Package

> A one-line description of what the package does.

## Docs

- [Getting Started](https://docs.example.com/index.html): Install the package and render your first site.
- [Guides](https://docs.example.com/guides/index.html): Task-oriented walkthroughs.

## API Reference

- [my_package](https://docs.example.com/reference/my_package.html): Core tasks and utilities.
- [my_package.utils](https://docs.example.com/reference/my_package.utils.html): Helper functions.

## CLI Reference

- [my_package run](https://docs.example.com/reference/my_package_run.html): Run a task.
```

### Page descriptions

Each entry in `llms.txt` uses the page's frontmatter `description` field as its summary. Writing a short `description` on every article and reference page is the single most impactful thing you can do to make `llms.txt` useful — both for LLMs and for the auto-generated reference index that uses the same field.

### Overriding with `llms-description`

Sometimes the summary you want to show *on* a page is different from the summary you want to expose to LLMs. The page description might be marketing-flavored ("Get up and running in five minutes!") while the LLM description should be more literal ("Installation, first render, and project layout").

Set `llms-description` in the page frontmatter to override just the `llms.txt` entry:

```yaml
---
title: Getting Started
description: Get up and running in five minutes!
llms-description: Installation, first render, and project layout.
---
```

If `llms-description` is absent, `description` is used. If both are absent, the page appears in `llms.txt` with an empty summary.

## Per-page Markdown sources

In addition to the HTML version of each page, `inspect-docs` publishes a plain-Markdown version at the same URL with `.html.md` appended. So a page rendered at `https://docs.example.com/guides/install.html` is also available at `https://docs.example.com/guides/install.html.md` as raw Markdown.

This is useful for:

- Linking an LLM directly at a single page without asking it to parse HTML.
- Tooling that wants the Markdown source for indexing, diffing, or transformation.
- Users who want to grab the source of a page to adapt it into their own notes.

## Copy page

To make the Markdown sources easy to discover, `inspect-docs` injects a **Copy page** button into every page header. The button lets readers:

- **Copy as Markdown** — copy the current page's Markdown source straight to the clipboard.
- **Open as Markdown** — open the `.html.md` version in a new tab.

The intended use case is pasting a doc page into a chat with an LLM: one click, no manual cleanup, no HTML-to-Markdown conversion required. Readers who already have an LLM workflow can use it; readers who don't never see anything unusual — the button is just a small icon that doesn't get in the way of the normal reading experience.
